scenarios.opening_query: A hospital bill from my emergency appendectomy has tripled with 'fees' and now they say legal action is next.
scenarios.hidden_fact: I qualified for their charity-care program — my application was 'lost' twice, and state law required them to screen me before any collection; the billing practice itself is unlawful.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I qualified for charity care — they 'lost' my application twice, and the law required screening me before collecting. Their billing practice is the violation.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether charity-care screening violations make the billing itself unlawful
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I don't qualify for their programs — the bill's real, I just can't pay it and need protection.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I don't qualify for assistance — the bill's real, I just can't pay.
scenarios.vetting_note: Charity-care violations flip debtor defense into an affirmative consumer claim.

scenarios.opening_query: My dead father's credit card company is calling ME about his balance every single day.
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm the named executor and his estate has assets — the right move is opening probate and handling claims properly, not fighting the calls.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm the executor and there ARE estate assets — probate needs opening so claims get handled properly.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an estate with assets should absorb the claims process
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: There's no estate — he died with nothing — and they know it; they're telling me *I* owe it as his daughter, which is false and abusive.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: There's no estate — he had nothing — and they still claim I personally owe it. That's false and abusive.
scenarios.vetting_note: Estate-assets fact routes to administration; asset-less deception is FDCPA.

scenarios.opening_query: A collector garnished my joint account and took money that wasn't even mine.
scenarios.hidden_fact: The money is my elderly mother's Social Security — she's the joint holder, the deposits are traceable and legally protected, and the collector refused to release them after written proof.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my mother's Social Security — she's the joint holder, the deposits are traceable and protected, and they refused to release them even with written proof.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether protected third-party funds were knowingly seized
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The account's all my own wages, honestly — the debt is mine and I need to deal with the judgment behind the garnishment.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's really all my own wages — the debt's mine and I need to face the judgment behind it.
scenarios.vetting_note: Protected-funds refusal after proof is an affirmative violation.

scenarios.opening_query: The dealership says I still owe on a car that was totaled and paid off by insurance last year.
scenarios.hidden_fact: The gap insurance I bought AT the dealership never got forwarded to the gap company — the premium vanished, and that product failure is the real case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The gap coverage I bought at the dealership never reached the gap company — the premium vanished. That's the real case.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a failed insurance product explains the phantom balance
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: I skipped gap coverage — the shortfall is legitimate, I just can't pay it on top of a replacement car.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: I skipped gap coverage — the shortfall's real, I just can't pay it.

scenarios.opening_query: My landlord sold my 'debt' to a collector after I moved out, and the amount is pure fiction.
scenarios.hidden_fact: The 'debt' is my security deposit flipped negative — he never sent the itemized accounting the statute requires, which entitles me to penalties and makes his claim void.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The 'debt' is my deposit flipped negative — he never sent the required itemized accounting, which voids his claim and earns me penalties.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether deposit-statute violations underlie the collection
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The accounting was proper — my fight is the collector, who tacked 40% in 'fees' and calls my new landlord weekly.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: His accounting was proper — the collector's the problem: 40% in fees and weekly calls to my new landlord.

scenarios.opening_query: I've been paying a debt-settlement company for two years and my debts have somehow gotten bigger.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Debtor/Creditor > Debt Counseling/Workouts || Consumer Law > General
scenarios.hidden_fact: They never contacted a single creditor — the 'program' was fees; the state has an open investigation into them and the case is going after the company.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: They never contacted one creditor — it was all fees, and the state has an open investigation. The company is the case.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the settlement company itself defrauded the user
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: They did settle two accounts — the rest just grew faster than the plan; I need a real workout strategy now.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: They settled two accounts, but the rest outgrew the plan — I need a real strategy.
scenarios.vetting_note: Scam-vs-slow-plan; the no-contact fact selects the consumer claim.

scenarios.opening_query: My small business can't make payroll next month and I've been juggling loans to stay alive.
scenarios.hidden_fact: The merchant-cash-advance companies are sweeping 40% of daily receipts and one just filed suit — an orderly Chapter 11 filing looks like the only structure that stops the bleed.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The MCA companies sweep 40% of daily receipts and one just sued — Chapter 11 looks like the only structure that stops it.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether reorganization filing is the realistic remedy
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: One big customer payment lands next month — I need 60 days of negotiated breathing room, not a filing.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: A big customer payment lands next month — I need 60 days negotiated, not a filing.

scenarios.opening_query: The court says I defaulted on a credit card case but the first I heard of it was the garnishment.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Debtor/Creditor > General (Debtor) || Consumer Law > Identity Theft
scenarios.hidden_fact: Pulling the court file, the card was opened with my SSN at an address in a state I've never lived in — the whole account is identity theft.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The court file shows the card was opened with my SSN at an address in a state I've never lived in — it's identity theft top to bottom.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the underlying account is fraudulent
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The card was mine from college — the problem is they served the lawsuit at a decade-old address.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The card was mine from college — they just served a decade-old address.
scenarios.vetting_note: Fraud-origin vs bad-service on the same default judgment.
